coredata.dc:title: Smart pH-responsive drug release systems based on functionalized chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels
coredata.dc:creator: Shariatinia, Zahra
Ziba, Mahdieh
coredata.dc:description: Chitosan (CS) was functionalized by formaldehyde (FA), acetaldehyde (AC), formaldehyde-urea (FA-U) and acetaldehyde-urea (AC-U) and the synthesized materials were utilized as efficient smart pH-responsive drug delivery systems (DDSs) for the cyclophosphamide (CP) anticancer drug. For this purpose, polymeric nanocomposite hydrogels were fabricated using CS-FA, CS-AC, CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the filler as well as glycerol and the poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) as plasticizers. The highest swelling degrees were happened in the acidic solution (2150–4300%), medium amounts in neutral pH (620–1250%) and the smallest values were observed within the alkaline pH (260–435%). The release percentages in three acidic, PBS buffer and alkaline solutions varied in the range of 64.5–77.7, 49.7–61.2 and 37.7–46.8%, respectively, illustrating a pH-responsive drug delivery which was approved by the statistical ANOVA tests. The CP release was significantly boosted within about 2–5 h, burst release, whereas release of the drug was subsequently slowly increased in 24 h demonstrating a sustained drug release. The kinetics of the drug release was examined for all of the DDSs and it was revealed that the Korsmeyer-Peppas was the best model as it was well matched to the drug release kinetics. The CS-FA-U-CQDs-CP and CS-AC-U-CQDs-CP were introduced as suitable slow-release drug carriers. It was finally found that the CS-AC-U-CP was the most desirable DDS as it simultaneously displayed favorable controlled, pH-responsive and high amount of drug release which followed the US Pharmacopeia drug release in acidic solution.

head.ce:title: Smart pH-responsive drug release systems based on functionalized chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic), P.O.Box:15875-4413, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic), P.O.Box:15875-4413, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chitosan (CS) was functionalized by formaldehyde (FA), acetaldehyde (AC), formaldehyde-urea (FA-U) and acetaldehyde-urea (AC-U) and the synthesized materials were utilized as efficient smart pH-responsive drug delivery systems (DDSs) for the cyclophosphamide (CP) anticancer drug. For this purpose, polymeric nanocomposite hydrogels were fabricated using CS-FA, CS-AC, CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the filler as well as glycerol and the poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) as plasticizers. The highest swelling degrees were happened in the acidic solution (2150–4300%), medium amounts in neutral pH (620–1250%) and the smallest values were observed within the alkaline pH (260–435%). The release percentages in three acidic, PBS buffer and alkaline solutions varied in the range of 64.5–77.7, 49.7–61.2 and 37.7–46.8%, respectively, illustrating a pH-responsive drug delivery which was approved by the statistical ANOVA tests. The CP release was significantly boosted within about 2–5 h, burst release, whereas release of the drug was subsequently slowly increased in 24 h demonstrating a sustained drug release. The kinetics of the drug release was examined for all of the DDSs and it was revealed that the Korsmeyer-Peppas was the best model as it was well matched to the drug release kinetics. The CS-FA-U-CQDs-CP and CS-AC-U-CQDs-CP were introduced as suitable slow-release drug carriers. It was finally found that the CS-AC-U-CP was the most desirable DDS as it simultaneously displayed favorable controlled, pH-responsive and high amount of drug release which followed the US Pharmacopeia drug release in acidic solution.

Synopsis for the Graphical Abstract: Smart pH-responsive drug release systems were fabricated based on chitosan films functionalized by formaldehyde, acetaldehyde and urea. It was shown that the cyclophosphamide drug release was sharply increased in ∼2–5 h but the drug release was then slowly enhanced within 24 h.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan (CS) is a natural polysaccharide which can be produced through deacetylation of chitin extracted from the fungi cell walls and crustacean shells [1–3]. CS is utilized in diverse applications such as pharmacology, ophthalmology, nutrition and food industry, cosmetics, photography, artificial skin, biomedicine, anticoagulants, insecticides, tissue engineering, and water remediation [4–7]. CS displays exceptional biological characteristics including immunogenicity, biodegradability, biocompatibility, mucus adhesion, wound healing, antibacterial and anti-corrosive potencies [8–11]. However, chitosan films are very fragile and this is due to the CS cannot form flexible films [12–14]. Therefore, CS can be functionalized and/or blended with other materials in order to be able to form flexible films [15–18]. When CS is chemically modified, hetero-organic moieties are incorporated on its chains and result in improving the CS solubility which enhances its drug delivery performance [19–21]. Furthermore, the incorporation of functional groups on the CS chains improves its interactions with drugs and inorganic filler nanoparticles [22–24]. The existence of numerous NH2 and −OH groups on CS and its derivatives leads to its strong H-bonding interactions with biologically active molecules like drugs [25–27]. Hence, CS and its derivatives are recognized as exceptional materials for usage as pharmaceuticals’ carriers [28–30].

Cyclophosphamide (CP) is extensively employed in treatment of various kinds of cancers/tumors as it a very efficient anticancer drug [31–33]. It is a member of phosphoramide family which illustrates anticancer activity against leukemia, sarcoma, multiple myeloma, small cell lung cancer, breast cancer, lymphoma, neuroblastoma and ovarian cancer [34–36]. CP decreases or stops growing and spreading cancer cells in human body [37,38]. CP is adsorbed by both of the injection and oral administration methods and its distribution within the body equals 0.48–0.71 g/kilogram of body weight. This drug crosses the cell barriers to enter the cerebrospinal fluid [39–41].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted lots of attention and they have been developed as cost-effective carbon nanoparticles with diameters of <10 nm. Also, they have extensive applications in several fields such as catalysis, photocatalysis, photoelectrochemical catalysis, electrochemical and fluorescence detections, optoelectronic devices, bioimaging, biological sensors, chemo-luminescence, biomedicine and drug delivery [42,43]. CQDs reveal favorable properties like low toxicity, high stability, biocompatibility, reducibility, strong photoluminescence, electrical conductivity and water solubility [44,45]. Also, the CQDs can easily be functionalized by oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups at the surface which leads to their strong H-bonding interactions with biomolecules used in drug delivery applications [46,47].

The hypothesis in this work is to fabricate more controlled drug delivery systems through functionalization of CS so that the synthesized materials also maintain their pH-responsive capacities similar to the native CS hydrogel. Thus, the purpose of the article is to achieve novel nanobiomaterials as pH-responsive hydrogel drug carriers with more sustained drug release behaviors compared to the CS. As the CS has lots of amino NH2 functional groups, it can easily react with the formaldehyde and acetaldehyde to form the imine (C = N) bonds and then this functional group can react with the urea to form the urea HNC(O)NH bonds. The synthesis methods of the CS-FA, CS-AC, CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U materials are displayed in Scheme 1 . It is obvious that the number of amino NH2 functional groups are three times greater in the CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U than in pure CS material. This is very much important as it is intended to achieve highly pH-responsive drug carriers that are better than the native CS. Hence, the fabrication of CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U was the hypothesis to reach this objective.

To achieve these goals, chitosan (CS) was first functionalized by formaldehyde (FA), acetaldehyde (AC), formaldehyde-urea (FA-U) and acetaldehyde-urea (AC-U). Then, the functionalized CS materials (CS-FA, CS-AC, CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U) were utilized to fabricate nanocomposite hydrogel films by the solution casting method using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the filler. Furthermore, poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) and glycerol (as the plasticizers) were added into the functionalized CS solutions which increased flexibility of these CS-based films and decreased their stiffness and fragility. The anticancer cyclophosphamide (CP) drug was incorporated in the films and the pH-responsive drug release capacities and release kinetics of all films were evaluated and compared to find the most efficient and controlled drug delivery shuttle illustrating satisfactory drug release rate.

The materials including chitosan, poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO, molecular weight=900,000 g/mol), formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, urea, ethylene diamine, citric acid, glycerol, HCl, KCl, NaCl, NaOH, CH3COOH, KH2PO4, Na2HPO4 and dialysis bag, were prepared from Sigma-Aldrich and Merck Companies. The cyclophosphamide anticancer drug (500 mg vial) was obtained from a pharmacy in Tehran, Iran.

Synthesis of CQDs was carried out using a process reported in literature [43]. Briefly, 1 g citric acid was completely dissolved in 50 mL distilled water. Then, 0.4 mL ethylene diamine was added to the solution which was stirred for 10 min until the solution became clear. Next, this solution was poured to an autoclave which was heated for 9 h at 175 °C. After that, the autoclave was let to cool down to the ambient temperature to obtain a light brown mixture containing CQDs. The mixture was poured into a big Petri dish and dried for 48 h at 90 °C. The CQDs powder was achieved through crushing the dried material on the Petri dish.

Functionalization of CS was accomplished based on the method described elsewhere [48]. Briefly, 1 g of CS was dissolved in a solution of 5% CH3COOH in distilled water under ultrasonic irradiation. Then, 0.01 mol (0.30 g) of formaldehyde was mixed with the CS solution and stirred for 5 h to obtain the CS-FA. The CS-AC was achieved via addition of 0.01 mol (0.44 g) of acetaldehyde into the CS solution and stirring for 5 h.

In order to synthesize the CS-FA-U (or CS-AC-U) material, 0.01 mol (0.60 g) of urea was added into the CS-FA (or CS-AC) solution which was then stirred for 12 h to obtain a gel like mixture. After that, the mixture was dried in oven for 20 h at 90 °C to achieve the functionalized chitosan powder (see Scheme 1). It should be noted that the CS samples functionalized with formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, formaldehyde/urea and acetaldehyde/urea were named CS-FA, CS-AC, CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pH-responsive nanocomposite hydrogel films
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prepare nanocomposite films based on the functionalized chitosan hydrogel materials, the powder of each functionalized CS synthesized by the above-mentioned method was first dissolved in 5% CH3COOH in distilled water under ultrasonic irradiation. Then, PEO polymer was also dissolved in H2O and added into the functionalized CS solution so that the CS:PEO ratio was 70:30 wt.%. The reason for adding the PEO to the functionalized CS solution was improving the flexibility of functionalized CS films and decreasing their stiffness and fragility. Additionally, glycerol was added as a plasticizer (20 wt.% corresponding to the overall weight of CS and PEO) to the functionalized CS-PEO solution to further enhance the flexibility and film forming capability of functionalized CS-PEO solutions. It is noteworthy that the PEO is a high molecular weight polymer (molecular weight=900,000 g/mol) but glycerol is a small molecule with a molecular weight of about 92 g/mol. The PEO chains are entangled among the CS polymeric chains and act as the plasticizer in this way. However, the glycerol molecules can only form hydrogen bonds with the functional groups of CS and lead to the flexibility for the fabricated hydrogel film. Consequently, addition of both PEO and glycerol is necessary to achieve flexible films which also display a good resistance against tearing because they have a strong influence on the characteristics of the fabricated films.

Finally, the functionalized CS-PEO solutions were casted into Teflon molds and dried in oven at 50 °C for 3 days to fabricate soft films with smooth surfaces. The nanocomposite films were prepared by the above-mentioned method but CQDs was also added (10 wt.% corresponding to the overall weight of CS and PEO) to the functionalized CS-PEO solutions. Lastly, the drug containing films were attained via the same method but the anticancer drug CP was also added (20 wt.% corresponding to the overall weight of CS and PEO) to the functionalized CS-PEO solutions. Moreover, all of the CS-FA, CS-AC, CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U as well as their CP drug incorporated films were fabricated by the same method described for the preparation of the CS films.

The XRD patterns were acquired on INEL EQUINOX 3000 X–ray diffractometer by means of Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5406 Å). The ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectra were recorded using Perkin-Elmer, Lambda 45 spectrophotometer. The Fourier–transform infrared (FT–IR) spectra were obtained by Bruker spectrometer (the wavelength range was 500–4000 cm−1) and Attenuated Total Reflectance (ATR) method was used as samples were not transparent. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images were taken by Philips XL30 instrument to investigate size and morphology of samples. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis was obtained on EDX energy-dispersive X-ray analyzer, model XMU, VEGA-II. The Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was performed using Particle Size Distribution Analyzer (model: SZ-100z, range: 0.3 nm to 8 µm). The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) plus DTA (differential thermal analysis) of the samples were taken on TG-DTA SDTA 851e apparatus in air from room temperature to 800 °C (heating rate was 10 °C/min).

The swelling capacities of the CS based hydrogel films were examined in three solutions with diverse pH values, i.e. acidic HCl (pH=4), alkaline NaOH (pH=13) and neutral PBS (phosphate buffered saline, pH=7.4). Each film was placed into a solution of known pH amount. Then, in various time intervals, it was removed from the solution and dried using a filter paper. The weights of the film before and after the fluid uptake were measured to calculate the swelling percent using the formula given below [49], where W0 reveals initial weight of dry film and Ws presents weight of swollen film.
Swelling

The drug release test was performed using 0.1 g of each polymeric hydrogel film as the pH-responsive drug carrier containing the CP drug. The drug release rate and mechanism were evaluated using three solutions of diverse pH amounts, i.e. neutral, alkaline and acidic environments with pH=7.4, 13 and 4.0, respectively, to simulate oral, intestine and gastric, media. The functionalized CS-PEO films were placed within dialysis bags containing 1 mL of the release medium that was a solution of known pH. Then, the two ends of the dialysis bags were closed using clean dental floss and put into a beaker filled with the release medium. Next, the quantities of drug released from carriers to the solution were measured at various time periods using the UV–visible spectroscopy which indicated a peak near 210 nm [33]. The accumulative drug release can be calculated at any time using the subsequent equation, where [CP]t is the CP concentration (mg/L, ppm) released at time t, and [CP]0 is the total CP concentration (mg/L, ppm) loaded into the films.
Accumulative

Herein, CS was functionalized by FA and AC through the condensation reaction of NH2 groups of CS with the C = O carbonyl groups of FA/AC which formed imine (C = N) bonds and released H2O molecules. In fact, this is the famous reaction between primary amine and aldehyde which affords the imine (C = N) bond and H2O [48,50]. The CS-FA and CS-AC materials were then reacted with urea through addition reaction to synthesize the CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U materials to be applied as the pH-responsive carriers for the CP anticancer drug. Indeed, the main reason/purpose behind the functionalization of chitosan with formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, formaldehyde-urea and acetaldehyde-urea was to obtain more controlled drug delivery/release materials via functionalization of CS so that the synthesized materials also maintain their pH-responsive properties similar to the natural CS hydrogel. As the CS has lots of amino NH2 functional groups, it can easily react with the formaldehyde and acetaldehyde to form the imine (C = N) bonds and then this functional group can react with the urea to form the urea HNC(O)NH bonds. The synthesis methods of the CS-FA, CS-AC, CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U materials are displayed in Scheme 1. Obviously, the number of amino NH2 functional groups are three times greater in the CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U than in the pure CS material. This is very much significant as it is intended to achieve highly pH-responsive drug carriers which are superior to the native CS. Hence, the fabrication of CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U is the hypothesis to achieve this aim. Notably, the poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) and glycerol (as the plasticizers) were added to the CS-based films in order to obtain hydrogel films of higher flexibility because the pure CS has a very poor film forming capability and forms very stiff and highly brittle films. Therefore, to fabricate flexible films which were resistant to tearing, both PEO and glycerol were necessarily added as they had a robust effect on the properties of the films.

The synthesis of CQDs was performed using the hydrothermal process. Scheme 2 demonstrates the suggested mechanism for the CQDs synthesis which indicates a condensation polymerization reaction occurs firstly and after that carbonization happens by pyrolysis of carbonaceous macromolecules that results in formation of N-doped CQDs. Fig. 1 indicates a photograph of the CS, CS-FA, CS-AC, CS-FA-U, CS-AC-U, CS-CP, CS-FA-CP, CS-ACCP, CS-FA-U-CP, CS-AC-U-CP, CS-CQDs-CP, CS-FA-CQDs-CP, CS-ACCQDs-CP, CS-FA-U-CQDs-CP and CS-AC-U-CQDs-CP hydrogel films as well as the CQDs powder. It is observed that the transparency of the films is decreased and in most cases the film color becomes darker brown upon the CS functionalization and CQDs incorporation. Fig. 1q reveals a schematic illustration of oral administration of the designed CS-based CP nanocarriers for the cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The XRD patterns, Fe-SEM images and EDS spectra
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD patterns of the CP, synthesized CQDs, CS, CS-FA, CS-AC, CS-FA-U, CS-AC-U and their polymeric and nanocomposite hydrogel films fabricated with and without incorporation of the CP drug are presented in Fig. 2 . Furthermore, Tables S1-S3 present the XRD data of CS, CQDs and CP, respectively, including line positions, heights, d-spacing, linewidths and relative intensities. The XRD pattern of the CP drug exhibits several peaks that are positioned at 2θ values of 7.12, 14.23, 14.91, 15.52, 20.63, 23.94, 26.18, 26.97, 28.50, 29.20, 34.04, 35.05, 41.44, 43.27, 48.44, 56.21 and 77.06° It is notable that although the XRD pattern of CP has been reported in literature [51], its crystallographic planes have not yet been indexed. The XRD pattern of the CQDs displays a broad peak located near 2θ≈24° which corresponds to the diffraction from (002) crystallographic plane of the graphitic carbon [52]. The d-spacing (interlayer spacing) values are calculated using the Bragg equation (nλ=2d sinθ) for the (002) plane of the CQDs and the CP sharpest peak at 7.12° that are 0.372 and 1.242 nm, respectively. The Scherrer equation (d = 0.9λ/βcosθ) [53] is utilized to measure the average crystallite size, where λ stands for the X-ray wavelength, β is the full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of peak, d shows the average crystallite size and θ is the diffraction angle. Using this equation, the average crystallite sizes of the CQDs and CP are equal to 2.2 and 35.5 nm, respectively. Therefore, the quantum size of the CQDs is also confirmed by the XRD analysis.

It was reported that the XRD diffractogram of CS displayed a broad peak near 18° and three sharp peaks at around 9, 11 and 24° which indicated the CS had a semi-crystalline structure [54]. The broad peak near 18° was related to the amorphous fraction whereas the crystalline fraction included two crystalline hydrated and tendon form I phases (phase T with three peaks near 9, 11, 24°) plus hydrated form II (phase L-2 with three peaks near 7, 16, 17°). The T and L-2 phases are generally known as the stable and metastable phases, respectively. Herein, the XRD pattern of CS in Fig. 2 reveals a semi-crystalline structure as it shows a sharp peak at 7.30° and a broad peak at 2θ = 21.25° associated with the crystalline and amorphous phases, respectively. Notably, the XRD pattern of CS exhibits that the broad peak has a very big surface area confirming the CS is mostly amorphous in nature. Also, the peaks located at 7.30 and 21.25° are related to the reflections from 020 to 101 planes, respectively. Similar results were reported in literature and it was shown that when 90 °C temperature was used for the chitin deacetylation to synthesize the CS, the XRD pattern exhibited five crystalline reflections at 2θ=9.3, 19.2, 20.0, 23.2 and 26.3°, respectively corresponding to the 020, 110, 120, 101, and 130 indices [55]. This XRD pattern was assigned to the chitin α-crystalline structure. As well, the intensities of the peaks at 020 and 110 reflections were decreased while the deacetylation degree was increased [55].

The XRD patterns of CS-FA and CS-AC present a broad shoulder plus a broad peak centered at approximately 7.8, 21.1° and 9.1, 21.2°, respectively. However, the XRD patterns of CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U illustrate a broad peak at about 22 and 23° plus some sharp peaks located at around 13.9, 22.1, 28.0, 36.0° and 11.3, 23.4, 21.3, 23.4, 26.5, 28.1°, respectively. Consequently, it is found out that the CS functionalization with the FA and AC increases its amorphous nature while functionalization of CS-FA and CS-AC with urea makes them semi-crystalline materials. Moreover, it is observed that addition of CP and CQDs does not change the XRD patterns significantly. This can be attributed to the relatively low amounts of CP and CQDs in the polymeric films. For instance, the sharpest peaks within the XRD patterns of the CS-FA-U, CS-FA-U-CP and CS-FA-U-CQDs-CP appear at 22.1, 21.2 and 21.2° but those of the CS-AC-U, CS-AC-U-CP and CS-AC-U-CQDs-CP are seen at 23.4, 21.3 and 21.3°, respectively. In both series, the sharpest peaks slightly shift to the lower diffraction angles which confirm the d-spacing values that represent the spaces between the polymeric chains are increased according to the Bragg equation.

Fig. 3 exhibits the DLS diagram of the CQDs powder as well as the FE-SEM micrographs of CQDs, CS, CS-FA, CS-AC, CS-FA-U, CS-AC-U and their polymeric and nanocomposite hydrogel films fabricated with and without adding the CP drug. The DLS analysis is achieved to evaluate the particle size of the CQDs dispersed in water. It is seen in Fig. 3a that the CQDs reveal the average diameter of 8.5 nm which is greater than that measured from the XRD pattern by the Scherrer formula. This can be correlated to the hydration/swelling of the particles in water. The FE-SEM image of the synthesized CQDs (Fig. 3b) indicates that they have nearly a uniform particle size (<10 nm) and highly regular spherical morphology which are not agglomerated.

The FE-SEM images of the CS, CS-FA and CS-AC reveal that the surfaces of these films are very smooth but the smoothness of the film surface is decreased upon functionalization with FA and AC. The surfaces of CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U films are not smooth which can be related to the attachment of urea to the CS-FA and CS-AC materials. It is observed that incorporation of the CP drug into the CS-CP, CS-FA-CP, CS-ACCP, CS-FA-U-CP and CS-AC-U-CP enhances the surface roughness degrees of these films. A similar trend is seen in the FE-SEM images of the CS-CQDs-CP, CS-FA-CQDs-CP, CS-ACCQDs-CP, CS-FA-U-CQDs-CP and CS-AC-U-CQDs-CP films containing both of the CQDs and CP drug.

The EDS spectra and the elemental mapping images were achieved to investigate the elemental compositions of the nanocomposite films. Figs. 4 a and 4b illustrate the EDS spectrum and the elemental mapping images of the CS-FA-U-CQDs-CP film, respectively, displaying the existence and homogenous distributions of the C, O, N, P and Cl elements in this material. Furthermore, it is observed in the EDS spectrum and the elemental mapping images of the CS-AC-U-CQDs film (Fig. 5 a and b) that the C, O, N, P and Cl elements are present and evenly distributed within the CS-AC-U-CQDs-CP nanocomposite film.

The UV-visible spectra of CP and CS-based hydrogel films are given in Fig. 6 . It is observed that the CP drug reveals an absorption peak near 210 nm within the UV region which is associated with the n→π∗ and/or π→π∗ transitions. The CS has very broad peaks with three maxima at around 215, 310 and 325 nm which are observed in the range of about 200–500 nm. The absorption peaks of the functionalized CS-FA, CS-AC, CS-FA-CP and CS-ACCP are extended to nearly 550–600 nm in the visible region. This can be attributed to both of the CS functionalization and incorporation of CP drug into the films. Besides, intensity of the absorption peaks is enhanced through functionalization of CS-FA and CS-AC with urea and also by adding the CP drug and the CQDs to the films. This result is in agreement with the Beer-Lambert law as the concentration is proportional to the intensity of light absorption.

The FT-IR spectra of CP, CQDs, CS and CS-based films are presented in the Fig. 7 . The CS exhibits a broad peak in the range of nearly 3000–3600 cm−1 that corresponds to the amino and hydroxyl (NH and OH) bonds occurring on its chains. The band at ∼2890 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of CH bonds. The peak located at about 1637 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of the amidic C = O (carbonyl) groups existing onto the CS chains with some acetylation degree. A band at 1554 cm−1 is related to the bending vibration of NH groups. The high intensity peak at around 1407 cm−1 corresponds to symmetrical deformation of CH bond. Additionally, the peaks observed at 1337 and 1103 cm–1 are correlated to the stretching vibrations of CN and CO bonds whereas those detected at 1030, 660 and 842 cm–1 are associated with the stretching and bending vibrations of CO-C, C–C and CH bonds, respectively. Such data are in agreement with the results described elsewhere [56–58].

The FT-IR spectra of CS-FA and CS-AC do not change very much compared to that of the CS but the characteristics peaks confirming the formation of the C = N bonds appear as new bands at 1652 and 1648 cm–1, respectively. Similarly, it was reported that the imine bonds illustrated the FTIR band at 1622 cm–1 [59]. As well, the FT-IR spectra of CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U reveal some additional bands in comparison to the spectra of CS-FA and CS-AC, respectively. For instance, the characteristics bands approving the formation of the urea HNC(O)NH bonds are observed as 1686 and 1670 cm–1, respectively in the spectra of CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U. The reason for this result can be attributed to the attachment of urea to the polymeric chains which changes the intermolecular interactions and increases the number of bands.

The amino groups of CP drug reveal a band near 3450 cm−1 due to their stretching vibrations. The stretching vibration of P = O bond is seen at 1225 cm−1. The stretching vibrations of P–O and P–N bonds appear at 1095 and 1045 cm−1, respectively. The stretching vibrations of CO, CN, CCl and CC bonds can be found at about and below 1000 cm−1. Moreover, CH bonds exhibit the stretching vibrations at around 2895 and 2970 cm−1. Similar results have been reported in literature [60,61]. Although the FT-IR spectrum of CP illustrates numerous bands, the spectra of CS-CP, CS-FA-CP and CS-ACCP are not crowded and they are very similar to that of the CS. This can be related to the presence of relatively low amount of CP in these polymeric films.

The FT-IR spectrum of CQDs demonstrates a broad band in the range of 3100–3700 cm−1 which corresponds to the stretching vibrations of the NH and OH bonds. The weak peaks at around 2850–3000 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of the CH bonds. The two sharp bands near 1550 and 1650 cm−1 can be correlated to the stretching vibrations of the C = O, C = N and C = C bonds. The band located at 1386 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of the CN bonds. The bands at about and below 1000 cm−1 can be associated with the stretching and bending vibrations of the CO, CN and CC bonds. It is obvious that the FT-IR spectrum of CS-CQDs-CP does not vary significantly in comparison to that of the CS and/or CS-CP. Nonetheless, the FT-IR spectra of the CS-FA-CQDs-CP, CS-ACCQDs-CP, CS-FA-U-CQDs-CP and CS-ACCQDs-U-CP display several additional bands plus some changes in the positions and intensities of the bands compared to those of their corresponding polymeric materials which are free of CQDs. This phenomenon can be correlated to the strong interactions of the CQDs and CP with the CS-FA, CS-AC, CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U polymers.

The TGA analysis was done to examine the thermal behaviors of the CS-based hydrogel samples. Fig. 8 presents the TGA and the DTA plots for the CS, CS-FA, CS-FA-U, CS-AC and CS-AC-U materials. It is observed that all of the materials reveal 100% weight losses except for the CS and CS-FA with weight losses equal to 96.4 and 99.2%, respectively. Higher weight loss amount of 100% for the CS-FA-U, CS-AC and CS-AC-U compared to those of the CS and CS-FA may be associated with the enhanced amount of hydrocarbon content in these polymers. Also, CS, CS-FA and CS-AC depict weight losses in four steps but the CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U compounds demonstrate weight losses in six steps. The first weight loss in all of these materials is attributed to evaporation of water molecules from the pores and surfaces. However, other weight loss steps correspond to the decomposition of these CS-based materials. The DTA plot of the CS exhibits four peaks at 82.9, 293.7, 501.8 and 597.3 °C. Also, the DTA graphs of the CS-FA, CS-FA-U, CS-AC and CS-AC-U materials show several peaks related to the H2O evaporation and decomposition of the corresponding polymeric chains. It is notable that the number of decomposition steps is enhanced upon functionalization of CS-FA and CS-AC with urea. This result confirms that the additional step can be due to the decomposition of urea from the polymeric chains.

It is well established that the swelling/water uptake of polymer drug carriers greatly affect the drug release from these materials [62–64]. Consequently, it is necessary to evaluate the swelling/fluid adsorption of these compounds. The water uptake of the CS-based hydrogel films has been accomplished during 24 h in three diverse alkaline, neutral and acidic solutions with pH=13, 7.4 and 4, respectively, at 25 °C. Fig. 9 displays the swelling diagrams against time for the entire CP containing polymeric carriers. It is obvious that in the three solutions with different pH values, the swelling of each sample is enhanced to its highest amount but it decreases after a certain time. This result can be related to the dissolution and/or disruption/degradation of the polymeric and nanocomposite samples which occur much greater in acidic pH compared to what measured within the neutral PBS buffer and alkaline media. However, the films dipped into the alkaline pH reveal a light/gentle decrease in swelling, followed by an almost constant water uptake after they reached their highest swelling and finally they remain practically intact without any tearing and/or cracking. The highest swelling degrees happen in the acidic solution (2150–4300%), medium amounts in neutral pH (620–1250%) and the smallest values are observed within the alkaline pH (260–435%). Analogous data are described in literature for several CS-based fibers and films [65,66]. This characteristic is very beneficial in drug delivery systems which can release the drug on desired areas/tissues upon swelling of the polymeric film. Furthermore, the highest swelling rates are seen within the acidic solution so that various films reach their maximum values in the time range of 3.5–8.5 h while the highest swellings occur in neutral and alkaline media in the time period range of 6–9 and 8–9.5 h, respectively.

Fig. 9 illustrates that in acidic pH, among the pure CS and functionalized CS hydrogel samples, the lowest and the highest swellings happen for the pure CS and the CS-AC-U film, respectively. The water uptake order among the CS and functionalized CS films is CS-AC-U > CS-FA-U > CS-AC > CS-FA > CS. The reason for this trend may be attributed to the occurrence of abundant amino groups on the CS-AC-U and CS-FA-U which are immediately protonated within the acidic solution and highly enhance the water uptake. Furthermore, formation of the imine (C = N) bonds in the CS-AC and the CS-FA polymers leads to the easy protonation of the N atoms through coordinative covalent bonds between the nitrogen lone pairs and the H + from the acidic solution. The reason for the higher swelling of the CS-AC than that of the CS-FA (or higher swelling of the CS-AC-U than that of the CS-FA-U) can be correlated to the presence of methyl group on the CH3 CH moiety which causes electron donation to the N atoms and leads to their stronger protonation with the H + cations in the acidic solution plus H-bonds with the water molecules. Overall, the swelling degree in acidic medium follows the order: CS-AC-U > CS-FA-U > CS-AC > CS-FA > CS > CS-AC-U-CP > CS-FA-U-CP > CS-ACCP > CS-FA-CP > CS-CP > CS-AC-U-CQDs-CP > CS-FA-U-CQDs-CP > CS-ACCQDs-CP > CS-FA-CQDs-CP > CS-CQDs-CP. It is noticeable that addition of the CP drug and also the CQDs into the CS and functionalized CS polymers declines the swelling degrees. This may be associated with the strong hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions occurring among the drug molecules/CQDs and the polymeric chains. Therefore, the films containing both of the CP and CQDs reveal lower water uptakes compared to the samples only including the CP drug. The lowest swelling is seen for the CS-CQDs-CP sample which reflects the amino groups of CS are engaged in the strong H-bonds and electrostatic interactions with the functional groups existing over the CP molecules and CQDs that eventually decrease the water uptake.

The swellings of the films in neutral and alkaline solutions exhibit a similar trend which follows the order of CS-AC-U > CS-FA-U > CS > CS-AC-U-CP > CS-FA-U-CP > CS-CP > CS-AC-U-CQDs-CP > CS-FA-U-CQDs-CP > CS-CQDs-CP > CS-AC > CS-FA > CS-ACCP > CS-FA-CP > CS-ACCQDs-CP > CS-FA-CQDs-CP. The highest water uptake of the CS-AC-U and CS-FA-U may be associated with the existence of several amino (NH and NH2) moieties over the polymeric chains which can participate in the hydrogen bonding interactions with the H2O molecules that can greatly increase the swelling degree. Furthermore, CS has amino NH2 groups on its chains which result in high H-bond formation and swelling. The samples CS-AC and CS-FA containing imine (C = N) bonds do not have any amino groups and hence they cannot form very strong H-bonds with H2O molecules similar to the CS-AC-U, CS-FA-U and CS films. Therefore, the water uptake values are much lower for the CS-AC and CS-FA. Herein, addition of CP and CQDs leads to more intermolecular interactions among the functional groups on the polymeric chains and the CP/CQDs which result in lower swellings compared to their related polymeric films.

To find the most suitable polymeric/nanocomposite hydrogel film for application in controlled pH-responsive drug delivery, the CP release from all of the CS-based drug carriers are tested in three pH values of 7.4, 13 and 4.0. The reasons for choosing these three pH values is that they mimic the oral, intestine and gastric media which are neutral, alkaline and acidic environments, respectively. Moreover, according to the following reasons, these pH values are chosen. The saliva that basically forms the environment of the oral cavity is the resting or pooled saliva. Saliva has a pH in the range of 6.2–7.6 with 6.7 being the average pH. Resting pH of mouth does not fall below 6.3. In the oral cavity, the pH is maintained near neutrality (6.7–7.3) by saliva [67]. Within the duodenum pH is 6, it is between 7 and 9 in the Jejunum, and about 7.4 in the Ileum. Sodium bicarbonate released by the pancreas maintains pH levels [68]. It is known that the pH of gastric acid is 1.5 to 3.5 in the human stomach lumen [69]. Also, it was found that the median gastric pH was ∼1, increased to pH 4.5 with ingestion of the meal, and then returned to nearly pH=1 after 3–4 h of starting the meal [69]. It should be noted that the pH=13 is very higher than that of the intestine milieu (maximum pH=9) but we used very high alkaline pH=13 in order to examine the drug release behavior of hydrogels at pH values greater than that of intestine. The release percentage data in Table 1 indicates that the carriers are able to release the CP drug in a solution with pH=13 confirming they will release more amounts of drug within a solution of lower pH values (i.e. pH<13).

Fig. 10 displays the release of the CP drug from all samples examined during 24 h in acidic, neutral and alkaline solutions at 25 °C. Besides, the amounts of CP drug released from the CS based films in three environments are provided in Table 1. It is seen that the drug release is sharply enhanced (burst release) in nearly 2, 4, and 5 h in solutions with pH values of 4, 7.4 and 13, respectively; however, afterwards the CP release is very gradually increased with time indicating the sustained release behavior.

Fig. 10 shows that the films placed in the PBS buffer (with pH=7.4) and alkaline solutions depict more sustained release rates than those of carriers immersed into the acidic solution. This result can be related to the higher swellings of the pH-responsive films in acidic pH which lead to more drug release from the carriers. The release percentages in three acidic, PBS buffer and alkaline solutions vary in the range of 64.5–77.7, 49.7–61.2 and 37.7–46.8%, respectively. Thus, for all drug carriers, the maximum, medium and the minimum drug release amounts are attained in the acidic, neutral PBS and alkaline media, respectively. This is because the nitrogen atoms of amino groups on polymeric chains and the CP drug are altered as ammonium cations within acidic pH which cause swelling and dissolution of carriers plus high amounts of drug release. Nonetheless, in the neutral and alkaline solutions, the NH2, NH, OH and C(O)OH groups on the carriers get involved in the H-bond interactions with H2O molecules and/or OH¯ anions and result in moderate and low drug release quantities, see Table 1.

The amount of CP release from various pH-responsive films in acidic solution follows the order of CS-AC-U-CP > CS-FA-U-CP > CS-ACCP > CS-FA-CP > CS-CP > CS-AC-U-CQDs-CP > CS-FA-U-CQDs-CP > CS-ACCQDs-CP > CS-FA-CQDs-CP > CS-CQDs-CP. This order is similar to that measured for the water uptake. As well, the drug release trends in both PBS buffer and alkaline media are completely analogous to what observed for the swellings of the carriers. These results approve that the more the drug carrier swells, the more it releases the drug molecules into the solution. The highest and the lowest drug release amounts at pH=4 are measured for the CS-AC-U-CP and CS-CQDs-CP, respectively. It should be noted that the existence of numerous amino groups on the polymeric chains of the CS-AC-U-CP leads to its very strong protonation and H-bond interactions with the acidic solution which finally cause the highest CP release. Moreover, it is found that adding CQDs results in greater intermolecular interactions among polymeric chains, drug molecules and CQDs which hinder/decrease the swelling and drug release.

The highest drug release amounts obtained after 24 h for the CS-AC-U-CP in three acidic, PBS buffer and alkaline solutions are equal to 77.7, 61.2 and 46.8%, respectively. Nevertheless, the burst drug release measured after 2, 4 and 5 h for the CS-AC-U-CP in three acidic, PBS buffer and alkaline solutions are approximately 57, 51 and 39%, respectively. Comparing these results with those of the pure CS-CP carrier displays that the drug release from pure CS has a slower rate. For instance, the CP release from CS-CP film after 24 h in three acidic, PBS buffer and alkaline solutions are equal to 70.4, 59.1 and 44.7%, respectively.

It is noteworthy to say that one of the best ways for using such pH-responsive drug carrier hydrogels is oral administration because they are solid films which can be shaped and/or cut into desired sizes and orally administered. However, if this therapeutic method is used, the drug carrier passes through mouth to the stomach and finally to the gastrointestinal tract. Notably, we should not be worry about the wide range of pH values in gastrointestinal tract or to address/predict their effects on the delivery system because this drug carrier spends at least 2 h in acidic milieu of stomach and it will be degraded there and release its incorporated drug. If some drug carrier particles reach the gastrointestinal tract, they will also release their loaded CP drug there. Alternatively, we can use these pH-responsive drug carrier hydrogels in local cancer therapies through tissue engineering in which the hydrogel drug carrier is placed within/onto the cancerous area(s) to show its therapeutic effect(s).

Statistical analysis was done in three pH values to find out if there are significant differences among the pH responsive release profiles of vehicles with diverse compositions. Results of ANOVA tests in three pH values are presented in Table 2 . It is observed that in all cases, the P-values are smaller than 0.05 which mean the differences are significant, i.e. diverse drug loaded carriers have significant differences. In fact, the significance of a factor depends on the amount of P-value. The smaller is the P-value, the more significant is the corresponding factor. Obviously, the smallest P-value is measured for the acidic solution which is a favorable result. Also, the P-values are smaller than 0.05 in neutral and alkaline environments which confirm the drug release values from the carriers in these pH values have significant differences. As well, in all cases, the F-values in three pH conditions are greater than the Fcrit-value which mean the variances of all factors are significant and all of them have important effects on the release amounts. Therefore, the release percentages are significantly different for the ten vehicles in three pH values; thus, they can be considered as efficient pH-responsive drug carriers.

The acceptance criteria for the standard drug release amounts are defined according to the US Pharmacopeia [70]. These criteria are described based on the dosing interval (D) which can be considered as the test time so that the accepted release amounts are 20–50, 45–75 and >75% for 0.25, 0.5 and 1.0 D, respectively. Thus, among all samples, the CS-FA-U-CP and CS-AC-U-CP which exhibit the highest drug release amounts of 75.3 and 77.7%, respectively, after 24 h within acidic solution obey the US Pharmacopeia.

In order to choose the most effective DDS, the carrier should simultaneously demonstrate high amount and sustained/controlled drug release behavior [71,72]. Therefore, among ten drug vehicles, the CS-AC-U-CP film can be suggested as the most appropriate sample as it reveals the highest drug release amounts at three pH values plus a reasonable controlled and pH-responsive drug release and obeys the US Pharmacopeia drug release standard in acidic solution. Notably, the CS-FA-U-CQDs-CP and CS-AC-U-CQDs-CP are also suggested as slow-release systems that are suitable for the purposes requiring sustained drug release.

Different kinetic models are usually used to define the kinetics of drug release from its carrier [73,74]. Herein, the CP drug release kinetics is studied by means of Korsmeyer-Peppas, Hixson-Crowell, Higuchi, first order and zero order models. To choose the most appropriate kinetic model, the release data have been fitted to diverse kinetics models and the most promising model is selected considering the R² amounts.

The zero order model indicates the drug release from delivery systems with a slow drug release. Eq. (1) presents the zero order model in which Qt and Q0 reveal the amounts of drug released at times t = t and t = 0, respectively and k0 stands for the zero order rate constant (with concentration/time units). To obtain the zero order model, the drug release amounts have been plotted against the release time.

The first order plots are attained through drawing the log cumulative drug release amounts against time which afford linear lines with a slope of -k/2.303.

The Higuchi model accepts 5 postulates that are (1) the drug solubility is much lower than the primary drug concentration in its carrier, (2) the drug diffusion only takes place within one dimension and the edge impact is minor, (3) size of drug particles is so smaller than that of the carrier thickness, (4) the carrier swelling and dissolution are insignificant, (5) a constant drug diffusion occurs and finally (6) perfect sink environments are always present in the releasing solution. The Higuchi model can be shown by Eq. (3), where ft is the Fickian diffusion, Q displays the quantity of drug released at time t and kH stands for the rate constant. The Higuchi model describes the mechanism of the drug release by the Fickian diffusion of the CP molecules from the carrier pores towards the solution. The Higuchi model is presented via diagrams of cumulative drug release versus square root of time.

Hixson and Crowell model says that regular area of a material is proportionated to its volume cube root. The Hixson-Crowell model is given by Eq. (4), where Wt and W0 exhibit the remaining and the initial drug amounts within its carrier at time t; the constant κ (kappa) relates volume to surface. The Hixson-Crowell formula expresses the drug release from a carrier through changing its surface area and diameter. The Hixson-Crowell diagrams are given as plots of the cube root of drug release amounts remained in carrier against time.

The Korsmeyer and Peppas model (Mt/M∞ = ktn) evaluates both non-Fickian and Fickian mechanisms of the drug release. In this equation, Mt/M∞ indicates a fraction of drug release amount at time t, k reveals the rate constant; n shows the release exponent which is 0.45, 0.65, 0.89 or 0.95 according to the release mechanism for the carriers exhibiting cylindrical forms. Hence, 0.45≤n demonstrates a Fickian diffusion carriage, 0.45<n<0.89 illustrates a non-Fickian transport, n = 0.89 stands for a Case II (relaxational) transportation and n>0.89 explains a super case II transport. The Korsmeyer-Peppas plots are indicated as diagrams of accumulative drug release percentages against timen.

Figs. S1-S3 present various kinetic models for the CP drug release from the polymeric and nanocomposite hydrogel vehicles in three environments with pH values of 4, 7.4 and 13, respectively. Moreover, Tables S4 and S5 provide the R2 values extracted from the plots of the Korsmeyer-Peppas, Hixson-Crowell, Higuchi, first order and zero order kinetics. Comparing the R2 values with each other proves that the Korsmeyer-Peppas model with n = 0.45 demonstrates the highest R² values which indicates this is the most proper kinetic model for the drug release which follows the Fick's rule. It is observed that the R² values of all other models are smaller confirming they cannot be appropriate kinetics models.

Functionalized CS hydrogel materials including CS-FA, CS-AC, CS-FA-U, CS-AC-U, CS-FA-U-CQDs and CS-AC-U-CQDs were synthesized and used as smart pH-responsive drug carriers for the CP anticancer drug. A burst CP release was observed within 2–5 h (in different acidic, neutral and alkaline solutions) whereas a sustained drug release was subsequently measured which was slowly enhanced in 24 h. The CS-FA-U and CS-AC-U had the highest amounts and fastest rates of CP release in three solutions with pH values of 4, 7.4 and 13. The CS-CQDs-CP as well as CS-FA-CQDs-CP showed the lowest amounts and slowest rates of drug release in acidic as well as neutral, alkaline solutions, respectively. All of the drug release data highly obeyed the Korsmeyer-Peppas kinetic. Among all CS-FA, CS-AC, CS-FA-U, CS-AC-U, CS-FA-U-CQDs and CS-AC-U-CQDs functionalized carriers, the CS-AC-U sample demonstrated the greatest amounts of drug release. The CS-FA-U-CQDs-CP and CS-AC-U-CQDs-CP were suggested as appropriate slow-release systems. As the CS-AC-U-CP system not only exhibited a reasonable sustained and pH-responsive drug release behavior but also it illustrated the highest drug release amounts at three pH values, accordingly, it was introduced as the most promising DDS which was conformed to the US Pharmacopeia drug release standard in acidic solution.
